mnemonics: ANOVA is a NOVA-way to test for Variance Among groups
Remember the F-Ratio: F = (Variance Between Groups) / (Variance Within Groups). Think 'F' for 'Fraction' of explained to unexplained variance.
H₀ is for 'Ho-hum' no difference. H₁ is for 'Hooray! Hypothesis 1 - there is a difference!'
keyPoints: ANOVA (Analysis of Variance) is used to compare the means of three or more groups to see if at least one is statistically different.
The core calculation involves partitioning the total variance into variance between groups (treatment effect) and variance within groups (error).
The test result is an F-statistic, which is compared to a critical value from the F-distribution to determine whether to reject the null hypothesis (H₀: all group means are equal).
commonMistakes: Conducting multiple t-tests instead of a single ANOVA, which inflates the Type I error rate (false positives).
Failing to check the assumptions of ANOVA: normality of data, homogeneity of variances, and independence of observations.
Interpreting a significant ANOVA result as meaning all groups are different from each other; post-hoc tests are needed to identify which specific pairs differ.
